title: Did Evas distribution affect peoples understanding of it
display_title: Did Evas distribution affect peoples understanding of it
wikitext:
[]: Did Eva's distribution affect people's understanding of it?             Quote Post by V » Sat Oct 11, 2008 2:48 pm Consider: Evangelion is a series from the mid-90's. It's a series where you can't miss an episode or you'll miss a little hint about something bigger. It's not overly complicated, but you have to "pay attention". However, I hear that back in the "Golden Age" (through the early 2000's) most people generally saw it at conventions or through poorly made fansubs which got traded around at conventions. Even at the best of times, it was buying at conventions $40 VHS tapes with only two episodes apiece. I am openly surprised how many people I've met that say "I don't understand the series, but I'm a giant fan"....only to reveal that they haven't seen some episodes. Or more commonly, that they only saw a rough fansub years ago. Problem is that Eva came out before the big "anime explosion" a bit later nowadays you can just walk into a store and buy the entire series with official English dub and many of us just saw the whole series off of Adult Swim, once a week and thus in order, and dubbed. see the problem is I was reading this old Eva review saying that "it used to be a game to actually come up with intentionally the most CONFUSING explanation for the events of End of Eva, rather than actually trying to understand it" (dude, its not a Rorschach Test) and indeed, End of Eva wasn't dubbed for years, and in the maintime pirated copies of bad quality floated around. I mean, I have literally met people who watched UNsubbed original Japanese End of Eva...who didn't speak ANY Japanese....and who were then surprised that they "didn't understand it"! So, Eva's release history (being not so much ADV's fault as simply that it is an anime from the mid-90's) ; major problem? Not that big of a problem? Of course, the "casual fan" Adult Swim folks might not understand the series well, but simply because no one ever told them about the Director's Cut scenes or End fo Eva. As for myself, I just watched the series straight off Adult Swim then watched End of Eva dubbed into English (indeed, actually skipping the original final 2 TV ending episodes to go straight to EoE after episode 24, then getting to watching the TV ending months later) ....and it actually made a fair amount of sense for me. I mean as I've said before, it wasn't *that* different from the "Matrix" debate of: "Third Impact and Instrumentality will bring in a perfect world were all souls merge, but that isn't "real" and even though my actual life was filled with pain, it was "real", so I should stop being an otaku and start LIVING my life" that, while interpretable, still had an obvious "basic framework" to me but EVERY convention Q&A you go to there are people whose first question is "what did the ending mean?" is this truly because they didn't get to see enough of the series (or if they did, badly fansubbed) during its initial release?

[]:             Quote Post by Dartz » Sat Oct 11, 2008 3:01 pm The translation of the series can have a big effect on understanding of some scenes. Case in point, I have trouble believing the Ritsuko was raped theory from Episode 23 because the fansub I have interprets the line differently to the way most people here do. Whichever way the line goes, it makes it seem more like the questioning itself is what is degrading and insulting, and that's something that become pretty ingrained in my thought process. So then it can affect how a person understands what's going on. Unless you're a native speaker of Japanese there's always going to be something in the way of a difference between the English version and what was intended. A translation way use a slightly different nuance on a word that alters a meaning entirely, or it may be wrong.... Of course, something like EVA can also be heavily coloured by our own beliefs and personal ideals. We're experiencing this thing through our own subjective self-universe, a construct of our minds used to understand and comprehend an objective universe that is influenced by our own experiences, beliefs and ideals. Add that on top of translation discrepenacies, and differences in national mindsets, and there's plenty of room to have widely different ideas that seem perfectly obvious and valid to their proponents.

[]:             Quote Post by AyrYntake » Sat Oct 11, 2008 4:48 pm Ritsuko was raped?!

[]:             Quote Post by V » Sat Oct 11, 2008 5:29 pm AyrYntake wrote: Ritsuko was raped?!

[]:             Quote Post by Shin-seiki » Sat Oct 11, 2008 10:15 pm V, I'm a little mystified by your premise that NGE suffered from inadequate availability or distribution. It was one of the biggest anime titles of the nineties, and ADV's main cash cow. When I saw the title of this thread, I thought you would be talking about how the Director's Cut episodes weren't commonly available to western audiences for so many years, or how Mangle dragged its feet in getting EoE out. But as for the original series, I, like a lot of people, saw all of it back when ADV first released it around '97-98.

[]:             Quote Post by Zaiku » Sun Oct 12, 2008 3:39 am Well personally, I only started watching Eva this year, so I skipped al the bad translations and crap, I saw properly subbed versions of the Direcor's cut versions of the episodes. However, it does make sense that people could misunderstand things by bad fansubs and REALLY misunderstand watching it in a language that they have no knowledge of... Especially when its EoE, something that requires a LOT of interpretation.

[]:             Quote Post by V » Sun Oct 12, 2008 10:08 am Shin-seiki wrote: V I, like a lot of people, saw all of it back when ADV first released it around '97-98.

[]:             Quote Post by Lucretius » Sun Oct 12, 2008 12:39 pm I imagine the original Japanese audience must've been pretty confused by EoE, since the DC episodes came out much later. I mean, if you haven't seen the shot of Gendou's hand in 24', then you have to wonder what the heel he's doing to Rei. And Asuka's actions in P3II make little sense without the "You won't even hold me!" bit in 22'. And the fact that Kozo has the hots for Yui is made much more obvious in the DC version of 21 than in the original. Were these scenes included in the original, theatrical version of Death? I remember reading somewhere that most of the DC scenes were included in the original version of Death, but cut out of the video version.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Sun Oct 12, 2008 12:55 pm Lucretius wrote: since the DC episodes came out much later.

[]:             Quote Post by Lucretius » Sun Oct 12, 2008 12:59 pm So, the DC episodes were released on video before EoE? I thought the new scenes appeared in Death before the videos came out?

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Sun Oct 12, 2008 1:04 pm This sort of information about timelines of releases used to be on EM's site (might still be). Not sure about the ordering vis-a-vis Death, but since that too was before EoE, the real fans would have seen the new material already.

[]:             Quote Post by Lucretius » Sun Oct 12, 2008 1:15 pm I guess it was just us luckless Westerners who were confused.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Sun Oct 12, 2008 1:22 pm Yeah -- going from the ADV original DVDs (I got somewhat lucky and got into the watching video habit again after a 15+ year break at just the right time to pick up the release) to EoE, like I did, makes the coffee-pot scene refer back to something you just have to guess about.

[]:             Quote Post by V » Sun Oct 12, 2008 2:10 pm Mr. Tines wrote: This sort of information about timelines of releases used to be on EM's site (might still be). Not sure about the ordering vis-a-vis Death, but since that too was before EoE, the real fans would have seen the new material already.

[]:             Quote Post by Silent Lightning » Sun Oct 12, 2008 2:16 pm Truth be told, Kozo having feelings for Yui was a nice little subplot, but it didn't make all that much difference had we not known about it.

[]:             Quote Post by AyrYntake » Sun Oct 12, 2008 2:28 pm A point I would fiercely dispute, SL.

[]:             Quote Post by CorporalChaos » Sun Oct 12, 2008 2:32 pm Its not a nice little subplot, its the backbone of the entire show. See, Gendo is infertile, so Fuyutsuki brought in his old sensei to father his wife's child, because he knew they had sort of hit it off in their college days. So, Gendo played the part of father, but kept Fuyutuski around so he would know his son. Then after Yui was gone, he sent Shinji away, because he developed a fierce anger that he had nothing of his own from Yui except memories. This would explain two things, why Fuyutsuki is nicer to Shinji than Gendo, and Unit-01 having a horn (Gendo was really compensating for something) Evangelion is really just a trashy dark romance story with sci-fi elements.

[]:             Quote Post by AyrYntake » Sun Oct 12, 2008 2:40 pm WHAA?! Now wait a minute. Wait a minute there...you're saying that Gendo 's infertile and that Shinji is in fact Fuyutsuki's son? Where the HELL did you get that one from?!

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Sun Oct 12, 2008 2:44 pm AyrYntake wrote: Where the HELL did you get that one from?!

[]:             Quote Post by AyrYntake » Sun Oct 12, 2008 2:45 pm I should have thought so...
